skill.name: swift-development
skill.description: Guides Swift, SwiftUI, Swift Package Manager, xcodebuild, simulator, testing, formatting, code signing, and CI workflows for iOS and macOS development.
skill.summary: Build, test, troubleshoot, and ship Swift apps with practical command-line workflows.
skill.author: hmohamed01

security_audit.summary: Static analysis found many command, filesystem, network, and credential-access patterns. Review found no prompt injection or malicious exfiltration intent, but the skill includes shell helpers and documentation that can modify local projects, simulators, keychains, and Xcode settings when users run them.

medium_findings.title: Local Shell Helpers Execute Developer Toolchains
medium_findings.description: TRUE_POSITIVE for operational risk: the included scripts invoke Swift, xcodebuild, SwiftFormat, SwiftLint, and simctl against local projects. This is expected for a Swift development skill, but users should review arguments because these commands can create files, alter source formatting, run builds, and change simulator state.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The scripts directly execute common local developer commands. The behavior is legitimate for this skill, but the commands have real local side effects.

medium_findings.title: Credential and Keychain Handling Guidance
medium_findings.description: TRUE_POSITIVE for sensitive capability: the documentation shows macOS Keychain and certificate import workflows for code signing. This does not expose secrets by itself, but copied commands can access signing identities, provisioning profiles, and imported certificates.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The referenced content explicitly teaches keychain and certificate operations. It appears instructional, not exfiltrating, so the risk is medium rather than high.

medium_findings.title: Destructive Maintenance Commands in Documentation
medium_findings.description: TRUE_POSITIVE for user-action risk and FALSE_POSITIVE for root deletion: the flagged rm commands target Xcode, simulator, and SwiftPM caches under the user account, not root. They can still remove useful local build state if copied without understanding.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The commands are visible documented troubleshooting steps. They are not malicious root wipes, but they can delete local caches and indexes.

medium_findings.title: Privilege Escalation Examples Require User Review
medium_findings.description: TRUE_POSITIVE for privileged command presence: CI and troubleshooting references include sudo examples for selecting Xcode or restarting CoreSimulator. These are common macOS developer operations, but they should be reviewed before execution.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The sudo usage is explicit and semantically legitimate for macOS tooling. Elevated privileges create operational risk but no malicious intent was found.

low_findings.title: Hardcoded Documentation URLs and Package Examples
low_findings.description: FALSE_POSITIVE for malicious networking: hardcoded URLs point to Apple documentation, official GitHub Swift repositories, example package dependencies, XML plist DTDs, or placeholder dependency templates. No evidence found that the skill sends secrets to an external endpoint.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The URL context is documentation and package-template guidance. Static network hits are real, but review found no exfiltration behavior.

low_findings.title: Static Weak-Crypto Detections Are Swift Version and Placeholder Matches
low_findings.description: FALSE_POSITIVE for weak cryptography: many weak-crypto detections correspond to Swift version numbers, xcodebuild flags, documentation examples, or placeholder identifiers. No evidence found of MD5, SHA-1, or other weak cryptographic implementation being used by the skill.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: Manual context shows version declarations and command examples rather than cryptographic code. This makes the static high severity misleading.

low_findings.title: Relative Asset Path Flag Is Benign
low_findings.description: FALSE_POSITIVE for path traversal: the new package script resolves its own sibling assets directory with ../assets. The package name is validated before use, and this path is not used to read arbitrary user-selected files.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The flagged traversal is a fixed relative path from the script directory. User-controlled package names are constrained by a regex.

low_findings.title: Prompt Injection Review Found No Evidence
low_findings.description: No evidence found of override instructions, claims of pre-approval, requests to skip security analysis, or text formatted as higher-priority system instructions in the inspected skill files.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: Targeted inspection and phrase search did not identify prompt injection indicators. This confidence is high for the reviewed files but not a formal proof of absence.

dangerous_patterns.title: Command Execution Through Shell Scripts
dangerous_patterns.description: The skill includes bash scripts that run local build, test, formatting, and simulator commands. This should be disclosed because execution depends on the user environment and can modify local files or device simulator state.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The commands are direct and intentional. They are standard developer tooling but still represent executable behavior.

dangerous_patterns.title: Sensitive Code Signing Operations
dangerous_patterns.description: The references include keychain creation, keychain unlocking, certificate import, and codesign access configuration. Users should avoid pasting real secrets into shared prompts or logs.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The sensitive operations are documented plainly. They are not hidden or automated by the skill.

content.value_statement: Swift teams often lose time switching between Xcode, SPM, simulator, signing, and CI workflows. This skill gives Claude, Codex, and Claude Code practical Swift guidance and reusable command patterns.
actual_capabilities: Explains Swift Package Manager setup, package structure, dependency commands, and testing commands.
Provides xcodebuild patterns for building, testing, archiving, exporting, and resolving package dependencies.
Guides iOS simulator workflows with simctl for listing, booting, resetting, installing, launching, and screenshots.
Documents SwiftUI, architecture, concurrency, persistence, and testing patterns for modern Swift projects.
Includes local helper scripts for package creation, testing, formatting, linting, and simulator management.
Covers code signing, provisioning profile checks, CI examples, and common troubleshooting steps.
limitations: Requires macOS and Xcode command-line tools for most commands to work.
Does not replace Apple documentation for the latest platform-specific API details.
Code signing examples require careful handling of certificates, passwords, and provisioning profiles.
Scripts may need adaptation for project-specific schemes, destinations, and CI environments.

use_cases.title: Set up a Swift package
use_cases.description: Create a package, add platform settings, configure formatting, and prepare a testable project structure.

use_cases.title: Automate iOS build checks
use_cases.description: Use xcodebuild, SwiftPM, SwiftLint, SwiftFormat, and CI examples to create repeatable build validation.

use_cases.title: Troubleshoot local app delivery
use_cases.description: Diagnose simulator, signing, package cache, DerivedData, and archive export issues with targeted commands.

prompt_templates.title: Create a Swift package
prompt_templates.scenario: Beginner setup for a new library or executable.
prompt_templates.prompt: Help me create a new Swift package named <PackageName>. Include the recommended package type, platform targets, test setup, and formatting configuration.

prompt_templates.title: Fix a failing test run
prompt_templates.scenario: Intermediate debugging for local or CI test failures.
prompt_templates.prompt: My Swift tests are failing with this output: <paste output>. Identify the likely cause and give the exact xcodebuild or swift test commands to verify the fix.

prompt_templates.title: Design a SwiftUI feature
prompt_templates.scenario: Advanced app architecture and implementation planning.
prompt_templates.prompt: Design a SwiftUI feature for <feature>. Use MVVM, async/await, clear state ownership, and testable service boundaries.

prompt_templates.title: Prepare release automation
prompt_templates.scenario: Advanced CI, signing, archive, and export workflow.
prompt_templates.prompt: Review my iOS release workflow for <project details>. Create a safe build, signing, archive, export, and TestFlight checklist for CI.

output_examples.input: Create a Swift package for a networking library.
output_examples.output: A step-by-step package setup plan with platform targets, directory layout, dependency guidance, test strategy, and formatting checks.

output_examples.input: My simulator will not boot after an Xcode update.
output_examples.output: A prioritized troubleshooting sequence that checks available simulators, shuts down stale devices, restarts CoreSimulator only when needed, and avoids unnecessary data loss.

output_examples.input: Prepare my app for App Store export.
output_examples.output: A release checklist covering scheme selection, archive settings, export options, signing identity checks, provisioning profiles, and validation steps.

best_practices: Review every generated shell command before running it on a real project or CI machine.
Keep signing credentials, keychain passwords, and provisioning files out of prompts and logs.
Run formatting, linting, tests, and simulator commands on a clean branch before applying changes broadly.
anti_patterns: Do not paste real certificate passwords, API keys, or private provisioning data into assistant prompts.
Do not run cache deletion or simulator erase commands without understanding the local data they remove.
Do not assume sample scheme names, bundle identifiers, or destinations match your project.

faq.question: Does this skill write Swift code?
faq.answer: Yes. It can guide Swift, SwiftUI, package, testing, and architecture work through prompts and examples.

faq.question: Can it run Xcode builds by itself?
faq.answer: It provides scripts and commands. Actual execution depends on the assistant environment and user approval.

faq.question: Is it only for iOS apps?
faq.answer: No. It covers Swift packages, iOS apps, macOS apps, SwiftUI, SPM, testing, and CI workflows.

faq.question: Does it include code signing help?
faq.answer: Yes. It includes certificate, keychain, provisioning, archive, and export guidance for Apple platforms.

faq.question: What should users review before running commands?
faq.answer: Review project paths, scheme names, destinations, signing settings, sudo commands, and destructive cache cleanup commands.

faq.question: Which tools can use this skill?
faq.answer: The report marks it compatible with Claude, Codex, and Claude Code.
